metadata.title: Guideline on the evaluation of medicinal products for the treatment of irritable bowel syndrome (Rev. 1)
metadata.note: Replaces 'Points to consider on the evaluation of medicinal products for the treatment of Irritable Bowel Syndrome' (CPMP/EWP/785/97). Adopted by CHMP 25 September 2014. In effect 1 April 2015. Major changes from prior version: patient population changed from Rome II to Rome III criteria; primary endpoints changed from co-primary (global assessment + pain) to stool abnormalities + pain.

rules.rule_text: Rome III criteria are the current standard for defining the IBS patient population in clinical trials; alternative criteria are acceptable only if adequately justified by all available scientific data, with concordance between the chosen criteria and the most accepted criteria evaluated at time of trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: The Rome III criteria are currently widely accepted as the scientific standard, and are therefore also currently accepted as the standard of definition in the regulatory environment. [...] Applicants are therefore requested – in the definition of their patient population to be included into clinical trials – either to choose the current most widely accepted standard – or to justify the definition used in the development programme by all scientific data available, and by evaluating concordance between the chosen criteria and the most accepted criteria at the time of conduct of trials.
rules.exceptions: Alternative criteria acceptable with adequate scientific justification.

rules.rule_text: Sub-indications based solely on one aspect of IBS (e.g. 'pain only' or 'diarrhoea only' or 'abdominal pain in IBS' or 'diarrhoea in IBS') are not within the scope of the IBS guideline and are currently regarded as problematic.
rules.verbatim_quote: Claims based on the influence on only one aspect of the illness (e.g. for pain or defaecation abnormalities only) are not within the scope of this guideline. At the current time, sub-indications such as 'diarrhoea in IBS' or 'abdominal pain in IBS' are regarded to be problematic.

rules.rule_text: Patient selection in IBS trials must be based on both symptom-based criteria AND exclusion of relevant other diseases with similar symptoms; inclusion criteria should include a certain minimum severity level of symptoms and/or quality of life reduction.
rules.verbatim_quote: Due to the poor validation data available, and considering clinical practice, the selection of patients should usually be done based on both, symptom-based criteria and exclusion of relevant other diseases with similar symptoms. [...] It is recommended to select patients with a certain severity level of symptoms and/or reduction of quality of life representative for the usual 'consulter' population as part of the inclusion criteria.

rules.rule_text: A 10-14 day run-in period prior to randomisation is required to adequately determine fulfilment of inclusion/exclusion criteria; placebo treatment during this period is not recommended, and exclusion of placebo responders is discouraged.
rules.verbatim_quote: for all studies, a 10-14 days lead-in period should be part of the design, in order to adequately determine the fulfilment of the in- and exclusion criteria. A placebo treatment during this period is not recommended, and the exclusion of placebo responders is discouraged.

rules.rule_text: Lactose intolerance and other malabsorption syndromes (e.g. fructose malabsorption) must be excluded from IBS trials, based on dietary response history or a trial of lactose-free diet and/or respective testing.
rules.verbatim_quote: The exclusion of lactose intolerance and other malabsorption syndromes (e.g. fructose malabsorption) should be based on the response to dietary changes (e.g. lactose-free diet). This can be done by history taking, or if in doubt, by a trial of such a diet and/or respective testing if available and deemed appropriate.

rules.rule_text: Coeliac disease must be excluded in IBS trial candidates by appropriate antibody testing.
rules.verbatim_quote: Coeliac disease should be excluded by appropriate antibody testing.

rules.rule_text: Basic laboratory tests (blood count, electrolytes, liver enzymes), stool cultures, blood in stool, and exclusion of relevant systemic or GI inflammation (e.g. CRP and/or calprotectin) must be performed for all IBS trial candidates; positive faecal blood test or stool cultures lead to further evaluation or exclusion.
rules.verbatim_quote: Basic laboratory tests (blood count, electrolytes, liver enzymes), stool cultures, blood in stool and exclusion of relevant systemic or GI inflammation (e.g. CRP and/or calprotectin), should be done for all patients. Positive faecal blood test and stool cultures would normally lead to further evaluation or exclusion of patients.

rules.rule_text: Colorectal cancer and structural abnormalities must be excluded by endoscopy per national/international screening guidelines or per protocol; historical endoscopic examinations available per guidelines are sufficient; patients with known familial colorectal cancer syndromes (Lynch, FAP) must not be included.
rules.verbatim_quote: The exclusion of colorectal cancer and other structural abnormalities by endoscopy should be conducted either if any of the above laboratory tests yields a result of concern or be done according to national and/or international screening guidelines. [...] If not available, endoscopic examination before inclusion of the trial is necessary. However, patients with known familial colorectal cancer syndromes (Lynch, FAP) should not be included.
rules.exceptions: Historical endoscopy per screening guidelines is sufficient if available.

rules.rule_text: For IBS-D patients, bile acid malabsorption must be excluded by a therapeutic trial with bile acid sequestrants (e.g. colestyramine or colesevelam) within the last two years before inclusion, or such a probationary treatment must be performed before inclusion.
rules.verbatim_quote: Bile acid malabsorption/Bile acid induced diarrhoea: Patients with IBS-D should have received a therapeutic trial with bile acid sequestrants within the last two years before inclusion, or else undergo such a probatory treatment (e.g. colestyramine or colesevelam).

rules.rule_text: Concomitant drugs with analgesic action or specific effects on bowel function must generally be excluded during IBS trials; they may be allowed only as defined rescue medication if adequately justified, and must be clearly specified and evaluated as an efficacy parameter and for safety.
rules.verbatim_quote: During trials, the use of concomitant medication should be restricted. Drugs with analgesic action or with specific effects on bowel function should generally be excluded, and may only be allowed as specific 'rescue medication' if adequately justified. The rescue medication should be clearly specified and evaluated as efficacy parameter (and for safety).
rules.exceptions: Rescue medication with clear specification is acceptable if justified.

rules.rule_text: Antidepressants potentially used to treat concomitant psychiatric co-morbidity or IBS may be allowed if patients are on a stable dose of one single compound prior to study entry and maintained on that dose throughout the study.
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of antidepressants –medication potentially used to treat concomitant psychiatric co-morbidity, but also used for the treatment of IBS – could be allowed, provided that patients are on stable doses for one single compound prior to study entry, and are maintained on that dose for the duration of the study.
rules.exceptions: Antidepressants are only permitted for a single compound at a stable pre-entry dose.

rules.rule_text: Lifestyle and dietary measures for treating IBS must be stabilised prior to study entry and maintained throughout the course of a clinical trial.
rules.verbatim_quote: Lifestyle and dietary measures for treating IBS should be stabilised prior to study entry and be maintained during the course of a clinical trial.

rules.rule_text: Early phase development must include full pharmacokinetic documentation, evaluation of drug-drug interactions, and PK in special populations.
rules.verbatim_quote: The early phase of development should also encompass a full documentation of the pharmacokinetics of a compound, including the evaluation of drug-drug interactions and PK in special populations.

rules.rule_text: All candidate IBS drugs must be evaluated for dose-response relationship in Phase II (late exploratory studies); dose-response studies should reflect intended use (intermittent and/or continuous) and the IBS subtype selection.
rules.verbatim_quote: In the phase II of the development, all candidate drugs should be evaluated for their dose-response relationship. These studies should already reflect the intended use of compounds (intermittent and/or continuous use) and the selection of the IBS-subtype.

rules.rule_text: Pivotal IBS confirmatory trials must be placebo-controlled, randomised, and double-blind; an active comparator cannot currently be recommended as primary comparator but may be included as a third arm once a standard pharmacological therapy is established.
rules.verbatim_quote: Both types of treatment schedules should be investigated in placebo-controlled, randomised, double-blind trials. The inclusion of an active comparator can currently not be recommended, but may become adequate in the future, once a 'standard pharmacological therapy' is established. Even if such a 'standard agent' has been established, placebo will still be considered to be the most adequate and decisive comparator, and in such a case, it is recommended to include active control only as a third arm.
rules.exceptions: Active comparator may be acceptable as third arm once standard therapy is established.

rules.rule_text: Short-term intermittent treatment studies must be evaluated in repeated treatment courses of less than 8 weeks duration; at least one repeated treatment cycle must be documented.
rules.verbatim_quote: Short-term treatment intermittent use of compounds should be evaluated in repeated treatment courses shorter than 8 weeks. [...] At least one repeated treatment cycle has to be documented.
rules.exceptions: Duration can be shorter based on compound pharmacology (e.g. antibiotics or probiotics) with justification.

rules.rule_text: Long-term continuous treatment studies must be at least 6 months in duration; other study designs or durations must be justified for their ability to adequately assess long-term sustained efficacy, withdrawal, and rebound, as well as safety.
rules.verbatim_quote: Large, double-blind, parallel group, placebo-controlled clinical trials should be performed in patients intended or found suitable for long-term continuous use. [...] The duration of such studies is recommended to be at least 6 months. Other study designs and/or durations will have to be justified in terms of their ability to adequately assess long-term sustained efficacy, withdrawal, and rebound, as well as safety.

rules.rule_text: All IBS compounds must be evaluated for occurrence of withdrawal and/or rebound effects in studies reflecting intended treatment duration; a randomised withdrawal phase in at least one Phase III confirmatory trial is the preferred method.
rules.verbatim_quote: All compounds should also be evaluated for the occurrence of withdrawal and/or rebound effects in studies reflecting the intended duration of treatment, which is preferentially included in at least one of the phase III confirmatory trials. A randomised withdrawal phase in such studies is currently considered to be the best method to have available a full comparison between ongoing treatment, new onset of treatment, and withdrawal of the active compound.

rules.rule_text: The primary endpoint in IBS pivotal trials must be evaluated as a responder rate; a responder is defined as a patient fulfilling the response criteria for at least 50% of the observation time. Numerical evaluation of changes in scales is regarded as a secondary endpoint.
rules.verbatim_quote: The primary endpoint should be evaluated primarily as responder rate. The numerical evaluation of changes in scales is regarded to be a secondary endpoint. [...] A responder is defined as a patient who fulfils the response criteria displayed in the following for at least 50% of the observation time.

rules.rule_text: For IBS-D, the primary endpoint responder definition requires: abdominal pain score improved ≥30% from baseline AND ≥50% reduction in days with stool consistency of 6 or 7 on the Bristol Stool Form Scale compared to baseline.
rules.verbatim_quote: a) IBS-D: A responder is defined as a patient with an abdominal pain score which has improved at least 30% compared to baseline and who experiences an at least 50% reduction in the number of days with at least one stool that has a consistency of 6 or 7 (in the BSFS) compared to baseline.

rules.rule_text: For IBS-C, the primary endpoint responder definition requires: abdominal pain score improved ≥30% from baseline AND an increase of ≥1 complete spontaneous bowel movement (CSBM) per week compared to baseline.
rules.verbatim_quote: b) IBS-C: A responder is defined as a patient with an abdominal pain score which has improved at least 30% compared to baseline and who experiences an increase of at least one CSBM per week compared to baseline.

rules.rule_text: For IBS-M, unsubtyped, mixed IBS-C and IBS-D populations, and global development programmes: the primary endpoint responder definition requires a patient's global assessment showing the highest two improvement grades (7-point scale) or highest improvement grade (5-point scale), AND abdominal pain improved ≥30% from baseline.
rules.verbatim_quote: c) IBS-M, IBS-unsubtyped, mixed IBS-C and IBS-D populations: A responder is defined as a patient with a subjects global assessment of efficacy scale of the highest two improvement grades if a 7-point scale is used, or of the highest improvement grade if a 5-point scale is used, and with an abdominal pain score which has improved at least 30% compared to baseline.
rules.exceptions: Use of the global assessment is recommended (not mandatory) for global development programmes also intending to treat two or more subtypes.

rules.rule_text: A minimally required number of valid diary entries must be pre-defined; patients below this threshold must be counted as non-responders in the primary analysis.
rules.verbatim_quote: In cases of weekly evaluations of the primary endpoints a minimally required number of valid diary entries should be defined in order to be evaluable as responder, and define patients below this threshold as non-responders.

rules.rule_text: Where global symptom assessment is not included in the primary endpoint (IBS-D and IBS-C programmes), a global symptom assessment using a scale measuring both improvement and deterioration must be defined as the main secondary endpoint; secondary endpoints must be generally supportive of the primary endpoints.
rules.verbatim_quote: In development programmes, where the global evaluation of the symptomatology is not included as primary endpoint (choices a) and b)), a global symptom assessment should be defined as the main secondary endpoint. The choice of a scale measuring improvement and deterioration is clearly recommended. [...] Secondary endpoints in IBS are regarded to complement the evaluation of the primary endpoints and are required to be generally supportive of the primary endpoints.

rules.rule_text: Quality of Life must be evaluated using validated generic and disease-specific QoL scales as a secondary endpoint in IBS pivotal trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: The evaluation of Quality of Life using validated generic and disease specific Quality of Life scales.

rules.rule_text: Separate paediatric clinical trials are required to prove efficacy and safety in children (age 4-18 years); extrapolation from adults to children, including to adolescents, is not considered adequate.
rules.verbatim_quote: Separate trials should be conducted in children in order to prove efficacy and safety of drug candidates. The age range to be included in these trials is from 4-18. Extrapolation from adults to children – even to adolescents – appears to be questionable.
rules.exceptions: Neighbouring indication data (chronic constipation, childhood functional abdominal pain) may alleviate requirements for the extent of trial data in childhood IBS.

rules.rule_text: Paediatric IBS dose-response, dose-finding, and PK data must be generated in all age groups from 4-18 years; ideally separate trials in different sub-age-ranges (4-7, 8-12, adolescents) should be conducted based on ability of children to reliably express and rate symptoms.
rules.verbatim_quote: Dose-response/dose finding and PK data should be generated in all age groups from 4-18 years. [...] Ideally, separate trials should be conducted in different age ranges according to the children's abilities to reliably express and rate symptoms (or the caregivers to do so) and the subsequent restricted availability of reliable outcome measures (e.g. 4-7 years, 8-12 years, adolescents).

rules.rule_text: Paediatric IBS confirmatory trials must be prospective, multi-centre, double-blind, placebo-controlled, and randomised; a waiting list arm (delayed treatment) may be included as a third arm; withdrawal and rebound effects must be investigated or their absence adequately justified.
rules.verbatim_quote: In children prospective, multi-centre, double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomised trials are necessary, a third arm with a waiting list (patients randomised to delayed treatment) can be included into studies in children. [...] Withdrawal and rebound effects should also be investigated in children, or absence of such investigation be adequately justified.

rules.rule_text: Long-term safety data of at least one year must be collected in paediatric IBS trials, as IBS is considered a chronic disease entity even in children.
rules.verbatim_quote: As IBS is considered a chronic disease entity even for children, long-term safety data – of at least one year – have to be collected.
rules.exceptions: A study duration of 2-3 months may be sufficient for efficacy proof if long-term adult data are well-established; but safety data of at least 1 year remains required.

rules.rule_text: Developmental parameters of growth and maturation must be documented in all paediatric IBS studies; agents with potential effects on CNS pathways must provide safety documentation on growth and development of at least 2 years (which may be provided post-marketing).
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, developmental parameters of growth and maturation have to be documented in all studies. Agents for which a potential influence on these parameters could be suspected (e.g. those acting by CNS pathways) should present a safety documentation regarding growth and development of at least 2 years. Depending on the overall safety profile and mode of action of the compound, the 2-years data may be provided post-marketing.
rules.exceptions: 2-year growth/development data may be provided post-marketing depending on compound profile.

rules.rule_text: A relevant proportion of elderly subjects must be included in IBS trials to allow reasonable conclusions on efficacy and safety of a new compound in the older age group; narrowing the indication away from elderly requires special safety justification.
rules.verbatim_quote: The intent to include a population reflecting the epidemiology of the disease (in terms of prevalence), and thus including a relevant proportion of elderly subjects should be part of all future development plans. Studies, and the proportion of elderly people included, should be big enough to allow a reasonable conclusion on similarity or differences in the efficacy and safety of a new compound.

rules.rule_text: Late clinical IBS studies should aim to mimic the natural sex distribution of the target disease; it is not expected to be acceptable to narrow a marketing authorisation to one gender during initial MAA without strong justification; sufficient male patients must be included to allow conclusions in both genders.
rules.verbatim_quote: If in the early development programme no gender differences are detected or anticipated, it should be aimed at including a sufficient number of male patients to allow conclusions on efficacy and safety in both, men and women. The inclusion in late clinical studies should aim at mimicking the 'natural' sex distribution in the disease for the population anticipated. [...] it cannot readily be expected to be acceptable to narrow the indication to one of the genders during an initial marketing authorisation procedure.
rules.exceptions: Development for one gender only is acceptable if differential therapeutic response with greatly reduced effects in one gender can be expected.

rules.rule_text: A sufficient proportion of patients recruited in Europe must be included in IBS development programmes; if a development programme is planned in one country or region only, ethnic/geographic/cultural analysis per ICH E5 must be presented at MAA.
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, the inclusion of a sufficient proportion of patients recruited in Europe is considered necessary unless it can be demonstrated that no relevant differences to European IBS populations can be expected. If indeed a development programme in one country or region only is planned, the respective analysis of ethnic/geographic and cultural factors according to the requirements of the respective guidance documents (ICH E 5, EMA/CHMP/EWP/692792/2008) should be presented at the time of MAA.
rules.exceptions: Non-European-only programmes accepted if no relevant differences to EU population can be demonstrated.

rules.rule_text: Long-term safety data with an observation period of at least 12 months must be available for all IBS products; for continuous use, at least 12 months on active treatment is required; for intermittent use, at least 6 months on drug with at least 12 months total observation; safety data from IBS sub-populations do not automatically support authorisation in a wider population.
rules.verbatim_quote: it is necessary to have long-term safety data with an observation period of at least 12 months available in adequate numbers to accurately asses the safety of the medicinal product. For products intended for long-term continuous use, this will mean the observation of 12 months on active treatment, whereas for compounds with an intermittent use, the time on active drug can be reduced to a period of at least 6 months, with the documentation of at least 12 months of observation (whichever comes first). Safety data collected in sub-populations of IBS patients may not support authorisation in a wider patient population.
rules.exceptions: For intermittent use, 6 months on drug with 12 months total observation is acceptable.

rules.rule_text: The main safety evaluation focus in IBS trials must be on gastrointestinal events, especially induction of diarrhoea and constipation, and their serious consequences: bowel obstruction/ileus, electrolyte and water-acid base balance disturbances, hypotension, and syncope; CNS events are the main focus for centrally-acting substances.
rules.verbatim_quote: the evaluation of safety should focus on the induction of diarrhoea and constipation, and of their more serious consequences such as bowel obstruction/ileus and of disturbances of electrolyte-, water- and acid based balance, hypotension and syncope. The focus of the evaluations may, however, change depending on the primary pharmacology of a compound, e.g. for centrally acting substances, the main safety evaluation may be more adequate to be put on the evaluation of CNS events.
rules.exceptions: Safety focus adjusts based on compound pharmacology.
